item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC-BY-SA 3.0) due to it being from the American Museum of National History (according to this source and others), which does not allow for commercial use, AKA "NC". Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
pretty useful in preserving the life

That's not what designates Thaumiel class anomalies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: y’know this scp would be pretty useful in preserving the life of a high ranking official just in case. possible thaumiel yeah?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I apologize for the necropost, but I just would like to say that the end is fantastic. It makes you think that the d-class is effected when, in reality, the doctor is effected. Upvoted. One question though: What is Protocol Wanderlust?
It's mentioned a few times, but is never expanded on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the end one was the best
just for the sheer sake of the fact that they JUST DUN GOOFED

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This should be safe, and I am unclear why it even needs monitoring while inactive. Just keep people away from it's housing.

I think it would be perfect, if the radius were expanded to many miles. The idea that Wanderlust is common, to prevent reports of "imortal" people…thats intense that SCP fakes that many car crashes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If that's the reason for your downvote, I'll be honest, it's pretty petty. Valid, but petty. Anyway, no, it's not a plant that makes crash test dummies, it's a major German automotive company's plant.

And yes, for the purposes of this article, there are more than enough Foundation operatives embedded in major companies to uncover stuff like this.

I have done a minor edit to make clearer that it's not a factory for crash test dummies though. You're right, that wasn't clear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Umm sleeper agent in random German factory that makes dummies??? How many employees does your head-Foundation have?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me a bit of Cain, but IMO the damage reciprocation anomaly works better thematically for a crash test dummy than a biblical sumerian. (Wait, Cain has a metal skeleton too!)

chicken was taken to the mess

+1

item.title: Object Class: Euclid?
item.content:encoded: It seems to me that in terms of containment, this one is almost certainly Safe. If people are kept out of its radius, the properties don't come into effect and it will just sit there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was the line that made me like this SCP. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, I've always wondered if that amused anyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good job taking a concept which feels dangerously close to a cliche (a voodoo doll) and making it work. Those bits of terse, dry humour definitely helped - the result of the last test is in a way quite amusing and the "ape got released, dolphin got released, chicken got eaten" cracked me up for some reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ahh ok, I was thinking after losing one researcher they would have wanted to try figuring out who it imprinted on first. I can see the logic behind making sure there is no security breach though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry about the late reply. It needs to be destroyed (or rather, sufficient damage is to be dealt to it) because if it's imprinted and the person we thought it'd imprinted is dead but it's not reverting back, that means we have someone running around who is largely unkillable. We have no idea who it is exactly and taking our time finding out might give that person time to leave the premises. By dealing damage to the dummy, the person imprinted on is terminated and the dummy reverts back to its inert state.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am confused about the bit where the SCP needs to be destroyed if it hasn't reverted within 30 minutes. I see two ways to read it and neither seems right. one, that failure to revert would lead to an apocalyptic event, which the article does not allude to. The other being 'apply lethal force to' in order to cause the death of the unidentified personnel it has imprinted on, though that could lead to another accidental death of a researcher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Props for the Weird Science reference. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I'm not talking candlewax on the nipples…

Perhaps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Someone mentioned a sad-

Oh. Not the super fun kind.

Never mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You just described my favourite type of sadistic orgy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's about it. You ram it into someone the dummy's imprinted on and the dummy gets perforated, while your feels like someone's rammed a spike through his abdomen, but has no visible injury.

I consciously omitted a seperate test log page, because it would quickly devolve into a sadistic orgy of D-class directed violence.

Edit: Jeez, forget how your own damn SCP operates, why don't you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But still behead the dummy? And what about if you pushed a sharp rod through the stomach, would it stop at the surface but still put a hole through the dummy? Man, I think you need a Test Log page. I could do those for WEEKS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And how useful that protocol is to save the world from an XK class scenario (XTREME KAWAII)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
WEEABOO Protocol would be initiated to figure out why you had a katana in the first place.

And it'd probably just bounce off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm inclined to think it would bounce off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, +1. One question though. If you were to swing a katana at the D-class's neck, what would happen - would the blade pass through the D's neck without causing damage?

item.title: Re: One Question...
item.content:encoded: Suffering an injury that would sever a limb for the second time would have no effect on the dummy. Its limb would already have been severed the first time. Look at it like this: if, in an epic episode of abysmally bad luck you manage to get an arm ripped off by a rabid badger (vicious little bitches) and then get involved in a carcrash that would have ripped that same arm off, you couldn't lose that arm again.

item.title: One Question...
item.content:encoded: It says that if you suffered damage that would make you lose a limb while imprinted on the dummy, the dummy will instead lose the limb. But what happens if that same limb again suffers damage that would make it come off? Will the imprinted subject just lose the limb because the test dummy already lost it and can't lose it again? Or would nothing happen to the dummy, essentially making the imprinted subject immune to losing limbs?

item.title: Re: Ok, I need an explanation.
item.content:encoded: Ok, NOW it makes full sense!

item.title: Re: Ok, I need an explanation.
item.content:encoded:
Actually, pain AND trauma carries over from dummy to subject, only trauma carries over from subject to dummy.

Edit: I re-read the paragraph detailing the mechanics and it's confusingly written. For that I apologize. Like I said, it's meant to convey the fact that while imprinted, damage to the test subject transfers to the dummy, but the subject still feels the pain. Damage done to the dummy transfers entirely to the test subject, including pain.

item.title: Ok, I need an explanation.
item.content:encoded:
'Dr. ███-█████ was returned to his next of kin in a closed casket.'
Closed casket is used, as far as I get it, to imply it was desfigurated.
BUT the article states the object doesn't carry over injuries from dummy to subject, just the pain.
If they have that test log, means they know it CAN carry the injuries.
So either the last log is added to give a creepy feeling via implied gore, which feels tacky… or the document needt to be changed to explain it CAN carry over injuries if they're enough to cause death, or something. Else that mistake/discrepancy kills the SPC.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All tests using SCP-1114 must be authorized by a level 4 member of research staff would be preferable. Similarly, the next two sentences should also be much more imperative.

You have a point there. My first article is bound to be plagued by things like this.

DATA EXPUNGED? That just might be some useful information to have there. At the very least, it should be redacted, rather than expunged.

As I've said in other discussions of my articles, expungement is my nemesis. Each day I raise my fist to the air and scream: "EXXXXPPUNNNGGGGGEEEE!!!". That said, I'll edit it the article to take your suggestions into account.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tests using SCP-1114 need to be authorized by a level 4 member of research staff.

Tests need to be authorized. Guys, authorize the tests. Seriously. Guys.

All tests using SCP-1114 must be authorized by a level 4 member of research staff would be preferable. Similarly, the next two sentences should also be much more imperative.

Upon unimprinting the object's face plating reverts to its initial form and any and all damage done to it instantly disappears. This includes previously severed parts reappearing, even if previously removed from the test site or destroyed. The method by which the object reassembles itself is unknown at this time. If these events do not transpire within thirty (30) minutes of the subject having been declared officially terminated, [DATA EXPUNGED].

DATA EXPUNGED? That just might be some useful information to have there. At the very least, it should be redacted, rather than expunged.

D-93832 was allowed to recover from the effects of the sedative in his cell. Dr. ███-█████ was returned to his next of kin in a closed casket.
Conclusion: Testing protocol needs revision. We need to very carefully match the face plating against ALL personnel in the activation radius, not just test subjects.

>_<

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for the, "Wait… WHAT!?!" feeling I got there at the end of the last test. Cudos for faking me out!

By normal testing protocols, this should have happened to a Junior Researcher/assistant type. Damn doctors should know better! LOL

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've got a point there. It's not an editing holdover, more like a bureaucratic legend to explain the following test log's format. Only I didn't put in what the fields should contain, so it's f**king useless really. I'm going to expunge it from history in a moment. Thanks for pointing it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the SCP, but should the test log template be there in the middle? It looks like an editing holdover.

It's a bit too big to fall under rule 6 editing for someone to remove.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I rather like this one… I also see some schmuck trying to say "Hey, we should get these things imprinted on some MTF fire teams so they don't die on missions," only to get Stooge-slapped repeatedly while explained that the Foundation doesn't do that sort of thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's fed into a trash compactor? Also, the repetition is sucky, I'll look into that. And quoting new wave wins you points. Not sure if they're positive though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… my god. It's the Crash Test Dummy of Dorian Grey.
My upvotes, cease your talking and receive them!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty darn good, especially the inherent dangers as shown by that last test log. There are a couple repeated words ("at at", "Dr. Dr." can't you see I'm burnin' burnin'), but otherwise, this feels pretty solid. I do question that one Expungement where the limbs don't come back after 30 minutes. Nothing's put into that to suggest what might happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any specific grammatical errors you can single out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couple grammar stuff, and i think the "Scryzone" facial recognition thing doesn't really need the company name in it.

Good concept though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, much better. This is good.

And thanks for clearing that up, somehow that's just as unsettling as no injury/all pain. Very cool, hope this stays in the positives. I don't see any reason why not, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I don't respond to posts here, it's because I'm sleeping. GMT +1 here, so after day wrangling three kids I'm up for a little rest. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I edited the shotgun into just a firearm to dimish the whole 'WHOO LET'S FUCK THINGS UP' feel of the test a bit, since I felt it went from needle to shotgun far too fast as well. It needs to be sufficient trauma for readers to be able to visualize something horrible, but not too wild to appear like someone just went Herbert West on that D-class. If there's something that would work better, please don't hesitate to suggest it.

Also, yes, you're misreading it. If the subject is injured, pain does NOT transfer and physical injury does. If the dummy is damaged, the subject feels both the pain and experiences the actual injury. That means that the poor guy in the factory suddenly felt his face cave in and then felt his ribs puncture his internal organs as his chest was deformed when the dummy hit the crash barrier in that car factory in Germany.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, admittedly I really like the final test log, you take a fairly straightforward concept and throw in a "twist" that the reader hardly expects… but I feel the doctor ordering the dummy to get shot in the face with a shotgun is quite the leap from a needle prick in the last test log. I guess it's a question between how much of the set-up to the wham line you want to sacrifice and how much you want the scientists studying this thing to adhere to rational scientific protocol. Not enough to detract from the overall article though.

Also, harming the dummy only transfers pain to the bonded subject and not physical injury… so why was the doctor placed in a closed casket? I think the idea of feeling the sensation of a shotgun blast to the face and not dying is horrifying, and works better. Or am I misreading this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're right. I actually thought I'd taken the 'claw' out of there already for exactly that reason. Not that a hammer in general is much better. I've edited the article (but I haven't taken out the chicken - I've grown fond of that chicken).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I got a kick in the ass by Sorts for being a very blunt and unsubtle boremonkey when I first posted a sandbox link (of a different SCP attempt) and it stuck. The amount of edits means I'm still finding my way, but I'm sure I'll get it at some point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's so much potential for darkness with this concept. A very sick person who came into possession of this SCP could torture someone endlessly and never worry about their victim expiring from blood loss, head trauma etc. It's an interesting concept that could have taken a very dark turn, but didn't. I'm glad you refrained from it. Sometimes the reader can fill in the blanks much more effectively when they have the right ingredients. Glad to see the tests were toned down a bit, too. Never been a fan of GRIMDARK test logs. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps a creature that can show signs of bruising. Then a moderate blow with a Reflex Hammer. A claw hammer just seems to be not very clinical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We should really be able to upvote comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see what you're saying. Also, I could put "tickled by a guy wearing a pink neon bunny suit" where it now says "[DATA EXPUNGED]". Textbook wrongful use. *facepalm*. I'll edit it.

Edit: I'll leave your edit untouched, but as far as I have been able to ascertain "to all intents and purposes" is actually the more correct form, with 'for all intents and purposes' being permissible. I figured someone might catch on to it. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had a hard time thinking of a controlled way to inflict blunt force trauma. The idea was to have three different classes of creatures receive three different types of damage. If you know of something that could be used to induce controlled blunt force trauma, I'd be happy to change the article.

Also: *ding*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last part before the test log rubs me the wrong way for some reason. It adds nothing to the article for me.

Besides that, no vote. The writing is neat, but I'm not a fan of the idea itself.

EDIT: The [DATA EXPUNGED] in the 3rd to last paragraph just reads to me like "the object will continue to accrue damage until expiration of subject".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looks like I read this after some of the revisions were made. As it stands, I like this. Not quite sure what purpose was served hitting a chicken with a hammer, when the other two animal species tested sustained only superficial injuries, but otherwise this is a well done article.

You recieve 28 XP!
You got: One Upvote!
Crayne leveled up!
Crayne's intelligence has increased by 2!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As per your suggestion I already took out the good Dr.'s hammer fetish. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with you on the ice pick thing. I'll see if I can find a better way to say what I want to say there. Are the other specific things that need rephrasing?

On the use of the dummy in a crash test: I thought of that but it seemed to predictable as a collection story…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Vezaz makes a good point. Starting small and working your way up is a good way to ensure your research is methodical and as safe as possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the execution. The damage transference mechanic is interesting, but statements like "trying to insert an ice pick into subject's eye…" break tone. Imagine you really had this; how would you go about testing it? You would make small superficial cuts and such; these are scientists, not cartoon villains. For the real dramatic application describe how it was used in an actual crash test and [DATA EXPUNGED] a bystander.
